explanation: Second branchial cleft cysts are the most common branchial anomaly (~95% of cases), presenting as cystic masses along the anterior border of the SCM muscle, typically at the junction of the upper and middle thirds. The complete fistulous tract extends from the tonsillar fossa (internal opening) through the carotid bifurcation to exit externally on the neck. They enlarge during URIs due to reactive lymphoid tissue in the cyst wall. First branchial cleft anomalies are near the ear/parotid; third branchial anomalies open into the piriform sinus. Thyroglossal cysts are midline and elevate with tongue protrusion. Reference: StatPearls NBK499523.
stem: A 12-year-old presents with a cystic mass along the anterior border of the sternocleidomastoid muscle at the junction of the upper and middle thirds. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.text: Second branchial cleft cyst (most common branchial anomaly, ~95% of cases)

explanation: First branchial cleft anomalies are rare (<10% of branchial anomalies) and present near the ear or angle of the mandible. The key distinguishing feature is their intimate relationship with the parotid gland and facial nerve. Work classification: Type I (ectodermal only, duplications of the EAC) and Type II (ectodermal and mesodermal, containing cartilage, passing through or near the parotid). The tract courses from the EAC region through the parotid gland. Surgical excision requires superficial parotidectomy with facial nerve identification due to the close relationship. Recurrence is common without complete excision. Reference: StatPearls NBK499523.
stem: A child has a cystic mass near the angle of the mandible with a sinus tract intimately associated with the parotid gland. Which branchial cleft anomaly is this?

choices.text: First branchial cleft anomaly (distinguished by relationship to parotid gland and facial nerve)

explanation: Third and fourth branchial pouch sinuses characteristically present with recurrent acute suppurative thyroiditis, almost exclusively on the left side. The tract extends from the piriform sinus (hypopharynx) inferiorly to the thyroid gland. Left-sided predominance occurs because the right-sided fourth arch contributes to the right subclavian artery, while the left side retains a longer course vulnerable to anomalous sinus formation. Diagnosis requires barium swallow or direct laryngoscopy to identify the piriform sinus opening. Treatment is surgical excision, often combined with ipsilateral hemithyroidectomy. Chemical cauterization of the internal opening is an alternative. Reference: StatPearls NBK499523.
stem: A child presents with recurrent acute suppurative thyroiditis, exclusively on the left side. A tract is identified extending from the piriform sinus to the thyroid. What is the embryologic origin?

choices.text: Third or fourth branchial pouch sinus
Ectopic thyroid tissue in the piriform fossa

explanation: This presentation (toxic child, stridor, drooling, tripod position, thumbprint sign) is classic acute epiglottitis. The priority is securing the airway in a controlled setting (OR) with an experienced team, as complete obstruction can occur suddenly. Avoid agitating the child — do not attempt bedside laryngoscopy, throat examination, or IV placement until the airway is secured. The thumbprint sign represents a swollen epiglottis on lateral neck radiograph. After airway management, treat with IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone or ampicillin-sulbactam) targeting H. influenzae and other pathogens. Nebulized epinephrine is for croup, not epiglottitis. Reference: StatPearls NBK430960.
stem: A 4-year-old presents with high fever, stridor, drooling, and sitting in a tripod position. Lateral neck X-ray shows a thumbprint sign. What is the priority management?

choices.text: Secure the airway in a controlled OR setting with experienced personnel
Start IV dexamethasone and observe in the PICU
Obtain CT neck with contrast before any intervention

explanation: Posterior epistaxis (from branches of the sphenopalatine artery) that fails anterior packing requires posterior packing with a Foley catheter balloon or commercially available posterior epistaxis balloon. Patients with posterior packing should be monitored in an ICU-level setting due to risk of hypoxia (nasopulmonary reflex), aspiration, and cardiac complications. Bilateral septal cautery is contraindicated (risk of septal perforation). Coagulation should be corrected (reverse warfarin with vitamin K ± FFP/PCC) but shouldn't delay tamponade. Sphenopalatine artery ligation or endovascular embolization is reserved for failure of packing. Reference: StatPearls NBK435997.
stem: A patient with posterior epistaxis has failed bilateral anterior nasal packing. Bleeding continues posteriorly. What is the next step?

choices.text: Posterior packing with a Foley catheter balloon or posterior epistaxis device
Repeat anterior packing with additional gauze
Apply topical tranexamic acid and observe
Discharge with bilateral anterior packs and ENT follow-up

explanation: This presentation (unilateral throat pain, trismus, muffled voice, uvular deviation away from the affected side) is classic peritonsillar abscess (quinsy). Treatment requires drainage — either needle aspiration or incision and drainage — plus antibiotics. Needle aspiration is effective as initial treatment in ~90% of cases. Antibiotics alone are insufficient for an established abscess. CT is not necessary if the clinical presentation is classic but may help if the diagnosis is uncertain or if parapharyngeal/retropharyngeal extension is suspected. Immediate tonsillectomy (quinsy tonsillectomy) is reserved for recurrent PTA or failure of drainage. Reference: StatPearls NBK519520.
stem: A 19-year-old presents with unilateral sore throat, trismus, muffled voice, and uvular deviation away from the affected side. What is the diagnosis and treatment?

choices.text: Peritonsillar abscess; requires needle aspiration or incision and drainage
Retropharyngeal abscess; requires IV antibiotics and CT-guided drainage
Infectious mononucleosis; requires supportive care only
Ludwig's angina; requires emergent airway management

explanation: Acute bacterial rhinosinusitis (ABRS) is diagnosed clinically by: symptoms lasting ≥10 days without improvement, double-worsening (initial improvement then worsening), or severe onset (high fever ≥39°C with purulent drainage ≥3 days). First-line treatment is amoxicillin-clavulanate (not amoxicillin alone due to increasing resistance). Azithromycin is no longer recommended due to high resistance rates among common pathogens (S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, M. catarrhalis). CT sinuses is not indicated for uncomplicated acute sinusitis — it's reserved for complicated, recurrent, or chronic cases. Watchful waiting may be appropriate for mild cases but this patient meets criteria for antibiotics. Reference: StatPearls NBK441934.
stem: A patient has nasal congestion and facial pressure for 12 days without improvement. What clinical criteria support a diagnosis of acute bacterial rhinosinusitis?

choices.text: Symptoms lasting ≥10 days without improvement, or double-worsening, or severe onset with fever ≥39°C
Any nasal congestion lasting more than 5 days with purulent discharge
Sinus opacification on CT scan with headache
Positive rapid streptococcal antigen test with facial pain

explanation: Pott's puffy tumor is a subperiosteal abscess of the frontal bone secondary to frontal sinusitis with osteomyelitis. It presents as a fluctuant, tender forehead swelling ('puffy') and can extend intracranially causing epidural abscess, subdural empyema, or brain abscess. Despite the name, it is an infection, not a neoplasm. Treatment requires IV antibiotics plus surgical drainage (often craniotomy for intracranial extension). Chandler classification describes orbital complications of ethmoid sinusitis. Lemierre syndrome involves internal jugular vein thrombophlebitis from pharyngeal infections. Cavernous sinus thrombosis can complicate sphenoid or ethmoid sinusitis. Reference: StatPearls NBK441934.
stem: A teenager with frontal sinusitis develops a fluctuant, tender swelling over the forehead. What is this complication?

choices.text: Pott's puffy tumor (subperiosteal abscess of the frontal bone from frontal sinusitis with osteomyelitis)

explanation: Rhinocerebral mucormycosis is an aggressive angioinvasive fungal infection caused by Mucorales (Rhizopus, Mucor) in immunocompromised patients, particularly those with diabetic ketoacidosis. The hallmark is black necrotic tissue (eschar) from vascular invasion and tissue infarction. Non-septate (pauci-septate), wide, ribbon-like hyphae branching at 90-degree angles are pathognomonic. Treatment requires emergent surgical debridement (often extensive) combined with IV amphotericin B (lipid formulation preferred). Delaying surgery dramatically increases mortality (>80% without surgery). Voriconazole covers Aspergillus but not Mucorales. Correction of hyperglycemia and acidosis is essential. Reference: StatPearls NBK544364.

explanation: The probe-to-bone (PTB) test — using a sterile blunt metal probe to palpate for bone at the base of a diabetic foot ulcer — has high positive predictive value (89%) for osteomyelitis when positive in high-risk patients. This patient has a deep ulcer with tendon exposure and positive PTB, indicating a high probability of osteomyelitis. MRI is the imaging modality of choice (sensitivity 90%, specificity 82%). ESR >70 mm/hr further supports the diagnosis. Bone biopsy with culture is the gold standard for guiding antibiotic therapy. S. aureus is the most common pathogen in diabetic foot osteomyelitis. Reference: StatPearls NBK441914.

explanation: IDSA/IWGDF diabetic foot infection classification: Grade 1 (uninfected), Grade 2 (mild — infection limited to skin/subcutaneous tissue, erythema >0.5 cm but ≤2 cm, no systemic toxicity), Grade 3 (moderate — erythema >2 cm, or deeper than skin/subcutaneous tissue, or systemic stability), Grade 4 (severe — systemic inflammatory response: fever, tachycardia, hypotension, or metabolic derangement). This patient has ≤2 cm erythema, superficial infection, and no systemic toxicity = Grade 2 (mild). Mild infections can be treated as outpatients with oral antibiotics targeting gram-positive cocci. Moderate/severe infections require IV antibiotics and often hospitalization. Reference: StatPearls NBK441914.

explanation: Severe diabetic foot infections with gas gangrene, necrotizing fasciitis, or sepsis require emergent surgical debridement — delay increases mortality and limb loss. Broad-spectrum antibiotics covering gram-positives (including MRSA), gram-negatives, and anaerobes should be started empirically (e.g., vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam or meropenem). MRI should not delay surgical intervention when clinical signs are clear. Hyperbaric oxygen is an adjunct, not primary therapy. Amputation may be necessary as a life-saving procedure. Revascularization should be considered if peripheral arterial disease contributes to the infection. Reference: StatPearls NBK441914.

explanation: In children, hematogenous osteomyelitis preferentially affects the metaphysis because nutrient arteries form sharp loops before draining into sinusoidal veins, creating areas of sluggish, turbulent blood flow ideal for bacterial trapping and proliferation. Additionally, the sinusoidal endothelium lacks a basement membrane, and local phagocytic activity is decreased. The open physis in children prevents infection from crossing into the epiphysis (except in neonates where transphyseal vessels still exist). S. aureus is the most common cause across all age groups. The metaphysis of long bones (femur, tibia) is the most frequent site. Reference: StatPearls NBK532250.
stem: A 7-year-old presents with refusal to bear weight, focal bony tenderness at the distal femoral metaphysis, fever, and elevated CRP. Why does hematogenous osteomyelitis in children preferentially affect the metaphysis?

choices.text: Nutrient arteries form sharp loops with sluggish flow in metaphyseal sinusoidal veins, promoting bacterial seeding
The growth plate acts as a barrier trapping bacteria in the metaphysis
Periosteum is thinnest at the metaphysis allowing hematogenous spread
Metaphyseal bone has the highest mineral density creating microtrauma sites

explanation: Salmonella osteomyelitis has a classic association with sickle cell disease due to functional asplenia (from autoinfarction) and intestinal mucosal ischemia from vasoocclusive crises, which promotes Salmonella translocation. Important caveat: S. aureus is still the most common cause of osteomyelitis even in sickle cell disease — but Salmonella is disproportionately represented compared to the general population. Pseudomonas is associated with puncture wounds through sneakers. Kingella kingae is the leading cause in children 6 months-4 years. Group B Streptococcus primarily affects neonates. Reference: StatPearls NBK532250.

explanation: Sinus tract cultures do not reliably predict the causative organism in osteomyelitis — they correlate with bone biopsy cultures in only ~44% of cases (exception: S. aureus from sinus tract is more reliable). Bone biopsy with culture and histopathology is the gold standard for diagnosing chronic osteomyelitis and guiding targeted antibiotic therapy. This patient likely has implant-associated (contiguous) osteomyelitis, which often requires hardware removal or exchange in addition to prolonged antibiotics. Biofilm formation on implants is a major challenge. MRI (not bone scan) is the imaging modality of choice, though it can be limited by metal artifact. Reference: StatPearls NBK532250.

explanation: This patient has a high anion gap metabolic acidosis (AG = 140 - 105 - 5 = 30) with an elevated osmolal gap (measured 330 - calculated 290 = 40). The combination of HAGMA + elevated osmolal gap narrows the differential to toxic alcohols: ethylene glycol and methanol. Ethylene glycol is more common in suicide attempts (antifreeze) and produces calcium oxalate crystals in urine (and can cause renal failure). Methanol causes similar findings but classically produces visual symptoms ('snowfield vision'). Isopropyl alcohol causes osmolal gap but ketosis without acidosis. Acetaminophen doesn't cause HAGMA or osmolal gap. Winter's formula confirms appropriate respiratory compensation: expected pCO2 = 1.5(5) + 8 ± 2 = 13.5-17.5. Reference: StatPearls NBK482146.
stem: A patient presents with altered mental status. Labs: Na 140, Cl 105, HCO3 5, BUN 10, glucose 90, measured osmolality 330. What acid-base disorder does the elevated osmolal gap suggest?

choices.text: High anion gap metabolic acidosis from a toxic alcohol (methanol, ethylene glycol)

explanation: This patient has a non-anion gap (hyperchloremic) metabolic acidosis: AG = 142 - 116 - 15 = 11 (normal), with appropriately compensated respiratory alkalosis (low pCO2). The urine anion gap (UAG = Na + K - Cl in urine) differentiates GI from renal causes of NAGMA. A negative UAG indicates the kidneys are appropriately excreting ammonium (NH4+), pointing to GI bicarbonate loss (diarrhea). A positive UAG suggests impaired renal acidification (renal tubular acidosis). Diarrhea causes bicarbonate loss from the GI tract with compensatory chloride retention (hyperchloremic acidosis) and hypokalemia. Reference: StatPearls NBK482146.
stem: A patient has a non-anion gap (hyperchloremic) metabolic acidosis. Urine anion gap (Na + K - Cl) is positive. What does this indicate?

choices.text: Impaired renal acid excretion (renal tubular acidosis), as the kidney is failing to generate adequate urinary ammonium
Appropriate renal compensation with adequate urinary ammonium excretion

explanation: Systematic approach reveals a triple acid-base disorder: (1) Corrected AG = (140-100-13) + 2.5×(4.0-2.0) = 27 + 5 = 32 → HAGMA (elevated lactate confirms). (2) Delta-delta ratio: ΔAG/ΔHCO3 = (32-12)/(24-13) = 20/11 = 1.8 → >1.6 suggests a concurrent metabolic alkalosis (HCO3 higher than expected for the degree of AG elevation). (3) Winter's formula: expected pCO2 = 1.5(13) + 8 ± 2 = 25.5-29.5, but actual pCO2 is 24 → lower than expected = additional respiratory alkalosis. Always correct AG for albumin: for every 1 g/dL below 4, add 2.5 to the calculated AG. Reference: StatPearls NBK482146.
stem: A critically ill patient has pH 7.18, pCO2 25, HCO3 10, Na 140, Cl 100, albumin 2.0, lactate 8.0. Calculated AG is 30 (corrected for albumin). Delta-delta ratio (ΔAG/ΔHCO3) = 18/14 = 1.3. How many acid-base disorders are present?

choices.text: At least a triple disorder: HAGMA (elevated lactate) + respiratory alkalosis (low pCO2) and the delta ratio suggests a concurrent process
Simple high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
Two disorders: metabolic acidosis and metabolic alkalosis

explanation: Brown-Séquard syndrome results from spinal cord hemisection producing: ipsilateral upper motor neuron weakness (corticospinal tract) and loss of vibration/proprioception (dorsal columns) below the lesion, plus contralateral loss of pain/temperature (spinothalamic tract) starting 1-2 levels below the lesion (because spinothalamic fibers cross at or near their entry level before ascending). This syndrome has the best prognosis of incomplete spinal cord injuries (~90% regain ambulation). The contralateral sensory loss begins below the lesion because the spinothalamic tract has already crossed. Anterior cord syndrome loses motor + pain/temperature bilaterally. Central cord has upper > lower extremity weakness. Reference: StatPearls NBK559117.
stem: A patient sustains a knife wound to the right side of the spinal cord at T10. Exam shows right leg weakness, right-sided loss of vibration sense, and left-sided loss of pain/temperature sensation below the lesion. What is the syndrome?

explanation: Anterior cord syndrome (anterior spinal artery occlusion) produces bilateral motor paralysis (corticospinal tracts) and bilateral loss of pain/temperature (spinothalamic tracts) with preserved dorsal column function (vibration, proprioception) because the posterior columns are supplied by the posterior spinal arteries. Aortic surgery is a classic cause because the artery of Adamkiewicz (the major segmental feeder to the anterior spinal artery, typically arising at T9-T12) may be compromised. While the artery of Adamkiewicz feeds the anterior spinal artery, the syndrome is named for the anterior spinal artery territory. This carries the worst prognosis among incomplete cord injuries (<10-20% motor recovery). Reference: StatPearls NBK559117.
stem: After aortic surgery, a patient awakes with bilateral lower extremity paralysis and loss of pain/temperature sensation, but preserved proprioception and vibration sense. What is the diagnosis?

choices.text: Anterior cord syndrome (anterior spinal artery occlusion)

explanation: Central cord syndrome is the most common incomplete spinal cord injury, typically occurring in older adults with pre-existing cervical spondylosis/stenosis who sustain a hyperextension injury (often from a fall). The narrowed spinal canal compresses the central cord, preferentially affecting the medial corticospinal tracts that supply the upper extremities (because motor fibers are organized with upper extremity fibers medially and lower extremity fibers laterally — somatotopic organization). This produces 'cape-like' motor weakness: arms >> legs. Sacral sensory sparing is common. Prognosis is variable — lower extremity function typically recovers before upper extremity. Reference: StatPearls NBK559117.
stem: A 72-year-old with cervical spondylosis falls and sustains a hyperextension neck injury. He has weakness affecting the arms more than the legs, with preserved sacral sensation. What is the most likely injury pattern?

choices.text: Central cord syndrome (most common incomplete SCI, upper extremities worse than lower)

explanation: Epidural hematoma (EDH) with significant midline shift and signs of uncal herniation (ipsilateral fixed dilated pupil from CN III compression) is a neurosurgical emergency requiring emergent craniotomy. EDH classically results from middle meningeal artery rupture after temporal bone fracture and has a characteristic biconvex (lens-shaped) appearance on CT. The 'lucid interval' — initial consciousness followed by rapid deterioration — occurs in about 20% of cases. While mannitol and mild hyperventilation are temporizing measures, they should not delay definitive surgical evacuation. Aggressive hyperventilation (pCO2 <25) causes cerebral vasoconstriction and ischemia. Reference: StatPearls NBK518824.
stem: A patient presents after head trauma with a brief lucid interval followed by rapid deterioration, ipsilateral fixed dilated pupil, and CT showing a biconvex (lens-shaped) hyperdense collection. What is the emergency management?

choices.text: Emergent craniotomy for epidural hematoma evacuation
Osmotic therapy with mannitol and repeat CT in 6 hours
ICP monitor placement and observation in ICU

explanation: In a stepwise ICP management approach, after positioning (HOB 30°, head midline) and ensuring adequate sedation/analgesia, osmotic therapy is the next tier: hypertonic saline (23.4%, 3%, or 5%) or mannitol. Hypertonic saline is often preferred as it does not cause diuresis (maintains intravascular volume) and has fewer rebound effects. Current BTF guidelines target ICP <22 mmHg and CPP 60-70 mmHg (CPP = MAP - ICP). This patient's CPP of 57 is below target. Decompressive craniectomy is a last-resort surgical option. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated with elevated ICP and mass lesion (risk of tonsillar herniation). Prophylactic hypothermia is not currently recommended for TBI. Reference: StatPearls NBK518824.
stem: A TBI patient has ICP of 28 mmHg despite head-of-bed elevation to 30°, head midline positioning, and adequate sedation. What is the next tier of ICP management?

choices.text: Osmotic therapy with hypertonic saline or mannitol

explanation: Cushing triad (hypertension + bradycardia + irregular respirations) is a late and ominous sign of critically elevated ICP with impending brainstem herniation. The reflex hypertension occurs as the brainstem attempts to maintain cerebral perfusion against rising ICP (Cushing reflex). Baroreceptor-mediated bradycardia follows the hypertension. Irregular respirations (Cheyne-Stokes, ataxic breathing) indicate brainstem compression. This triad is a medical emergency requiring immediate ICP-lowering measures. Importantly, it is a late finding — only ~33% of patients with critically elevated ICP display the full triad. Earlier signs include headache, vomiting, papilledema, and altered consciousness. Reference: StatPearls NBK518824.
stem: A head-injured patient develops hypertension, bradycardia, and irregular respirations. What does this triad signify?

choices.text: Cushing triad: critically elevated ICP with impending brainstem herniation
Neurogenic shock from cervical spinal cord injury

Posterior fossa hemorrhage with cerebellar tonsillar herniation

explanation: Ovarian torsion is a surgical emergency requiring prompt detorsion to salvage the ovary. Ovarian stimulation (IVF) is a major risk factor due to enlarged, heavy ovaries. Laparoscopic detorsion with ovarian conservation is preferred over oophorectomy, even if the ovary appears dusky/black — studies show >90% of ovaries recover function after detorsion regardless of appearance. Absent venous flow on Doppler (the 'whirlpool sign' of twisted pedicle) supports the diagnosis but presence of flow does not exclude torsion (intermittent torsion). CT delays treatment unnecessarily. The right side is more commonly affected (~60%), possibly due to the sigmoid colon limiting left-sided mobility. Reference: StatPearls NBK560731.
stem: A 28-year-old woman undergoing IVF stimulation presents with acute right lower quadrant pain, nausea, and a 10 cm right ovary on ultrasound. What makes IVF a major risk factor for ovarian torsion?

choices.text: Ovarian stimulation produces enlarged, heavy ovaries prone to rotation on the infundibulopelvic ligament
Hormonal changes during IVF cause increased peritoneal fluid reducing friction
Multiple follicular cysts create asymmetric weight pulling the ovary inferiorly

explanation: Dermoid cysts (mature cystic teratomas) are the most common ovarian neoplasm associated with torsion because their solid/fat content creates a heavy, pendulous mass that is prone to rotation on the ovarian pedicle (infundibulopelvic and utero-ovarian ligaments). Ovarian masses 5-10 cm carry the highest torsion risk — smaller masses aren't heavy enough, and very large masses are less mobile. Dermoids account for ~20% of ovarian torsion cases. Other risk factors include paraovarian cysts, pregnancy, and ovarian hyperstimulation. In children and adolescents, torsion can occur with normal ovaries (elongated utero-ovarian ligament). Reference: StatPearls NBK560731.
stem: A 22-year-old woman with acute pelvic pain undergoes laparoscopy revealing a twisted right ovary with a 6 cm dermoid cyst (mature cystic teratoma). Why are dermoid cysts particularly associated with torsion?

choices.text: Their solid/fat content creates a heavy, pendulous mass prone to rotation
Dermoid cysts have long pedicles that predispose to twisting
The calcium within dermoid cysts increases gravitational pull

explanation: Ovarian torsion is most common in the first trimester of pregnancy due to the physiologically enlarged corpus luteum (which produces progesterone to maintain early pregnancy until the placenta takes over at ~10-12 weeks). The corpus luteum can enlarge to 5-10 cm, creating a mass effect that predisposes to torsion. Additionally, the rapidly growing uterus in early pregnancy increases ovarian mobility. After the first trimester, the corpus luteum regresses and torsion risk decreases. Pregnancy is the fifth most common cause of abdominal surgery during gestation. Laparoscopy is safe in all trimesters. Reference: StatPearls NBK560731.
stem: A woman at 10 weeks gestation presents with acute right pelvic pain. Imaging suggests ovarian torsion. Why is torsion most common in the first trimester of pregnancy?

explanation: Per CDC guidelines, empiric treatment for PID should be initiated with a low threshold in sexually active young women with pelvic/abdominal pain if ANY ONE of the following minimum criteria is present: cervical motion tenderness, uterine tenderness, or adnexal tenderness. A low diagnostic threshold is used because the consequences of untreated PID (infertility, ectopic pregnancy, chronic pelvic pain) are severe. Additional criteria that increase specificity include: temperature >38.3°C, mucopurulent cervical discharge, WBC on wet mount, elevated ESR/CRP, and positive chlamydia/gonorrhea testing. Treatment does not require laboratory confirmation. Reference: StatPearls NBK499959.

Fever plus purulent vaginal discharge plus pelvic pain

explanation: A complex adnexal mass in the setting of worsening PID is a tubo-ovarian abscess (TOA) until proven otherwise. TOA is a serious complication of PID occurring in ~15% of cases. Initial management is hospitalization with IV broad-spectrum antibiotics — approximately 70% of TOAs respond to medical management alone. Standard regimens include clindamycin + gentamicin or ampicillin-sulbactam. If no improvement in 48-72 hours of IV antibiotics, CT/ultrasound-guided percutaneous drainage or surgical intervention is indicated. TOA rupture is a surgical emergency with risk of sepsis and requires immediate laparotomy. Reference: StatPearls NBK499959.

explanation: CDC-recommended outpatient PID regimen: IM ceftriaxone 500 mg (covers N. gonorrhoeae) PLUS oral doxycycline 100 mg BID × 14 days (covers C. trachomatis and other organisms) PLUS/MINUS oral metronidazole 500 mg BID × 14 days (for anaerobic/BV coverage, now routinely recommended). PID is polymicrobial — regimens must cover gonorrhea, chlamydia, anaerobes, gram-negatives, and streptococci. Patients should be reassessed in 48-72 hours. Hospitalization criteria include: surgical emergency cannot be excluded, TOA, pregnancy, severe illness, inability to tolerate oral therapy, or failure of outpatient treatment. Reference: StatPearls NBK499959.

explanation: Chemical eye injury is a true ophthalmic emergency where treatment (copious irrigation) must begin IMMEDIATELY — before visual acuity testing, before slit lamp examination, and before any other assessment. Alkaline burns (NaOH, lye, cement) are more dangerous than acid burns because alkalis cause liquefactive necrosis and penetrate deeper into ocular tissues (saponification of cell membranes). Irrigation should continue for at least 30 minutes (ideally using a Morgan lens) until the pH of the fornices normalizes to 7.0-7.4 (check with pH paper). Irrigation should not be delayed for ANY reason including transport, triage, or assessment. Reference: StatPearls NBK459283.
stem: A factory worker splashes an alkaline chemical into his right eye. He arrives in the ED 5 minutes later. What is the FIRST action?

choices.text: Immediate copious irrigation — before visual acuity, slit lamp, or any other assessment

pH testing of the conjunctival sac to determine severity

explanation: Acute angle-closure glaucoma (AACG) is an ophthalmic emergency requiring rapid IOP reduction to prevent optic nerve damage. Initial management: (1) topical timolol 0.5% (decreases aqueous production), (2) topical pilocarpine 1-2% (constricts pupil, opens drainage angle — but delay until IOP starts to fall, as sphincter paralysis at very high IOPs makes it ineffective), (3) IV or oral acetazolamide 500 mg (systemic carbonic anhydrase inhibitor), (4) topical apraclonidine (alpha-agonist). Atropine is CONTRAINDICATED — it dilates the pupil and worsens angle closure. Definitive treatment is laser peripheral iridotomy. The fellow eye should also receive prophylactic iridotomy. Reference: StatPearls NBK430857.
stem: A patient presents with severe eye pain, a fixed mid-dilated pupil, cloudy cornea, and intraocular pressure of 58 mmHg. What is the initial pharmacologic management?

choices.text: Topical timolol + pilocarpine + IV acetazolamide to rapidly reduce IOP (acute angle-closure glaucoma)

explanation: Central retinal artery occlusion (CRAO) presents with sudden painless monocular vision loss, a pale retina with cherry-red spot (the macula's choroidal blood supply is visible against the ischemic retina), and an afferent pupillary defect. It is essentially a 'stroke of the eye' — most commonly caused by thromboembolism (atrial fibrillation, carotid disease). The retina tolerates ischemia for approximately 90-100 minutes before irreversible damage, but intervention within 4-6 hours may still provide benefit. Emergent ophthalmology consultation and consideration of intra-arterial thrombolysis, ocular massage, or anterior chamber paracentesis are indicated. All patients need stroke workup (carotid imaging, echocardiogram, cardiac monitoring). Reference: StatPearls NBK470354.
